coredata.dc:title: Synergistic enhanced of carbon dots and eosin Y on fenton chemiluminescence for the determination of methionine
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yaqiong
Han, Suqin
coredata.dc:description: The ultra-weak chemiluminescence (CL) of Fenton system (Fe2+–H2O2) could be enhanced by phosphorus and chlorine co-doped carbon dots (PClCDs) or eosinY (EY), respectively. Surprisingly, the CL intensity of Fenton system was further improved in the co-presence of PClCDs and EY. The synergistic enhancing effect of PClCDs and EY on the CL emission of Fenton reaction provided a more strong and efficient CL system. It was found that methionine (Met) could effectively diminish the CL intensity of PClCDs-EY-Fenton system. Under the optimium conditions, a linear dynamic range of 0.1–10.0 μM Met concentrations was obtained, with limit of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) of 0.03 μM and 0.1 μM, respectively. The practical application of the presented CL system was demonstrated for detection of Met in food additive and feed additive samples. The mechanism of CL emission was described.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemiluminescence
Phosphorus and chlorine co-doped carbon dots
Eosin Y
Fenton reaction
Methionine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design of of PClCDs-enhanced CL system by FI mode
CL from PClCDs-EY-Fenton system by batch experiments
Role of PClCDs or EY in Fenton reaction
Synergistic effect of PClCDs and EY for Fenton CL enhancement
Selectivity of the proposed CL system

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 2013011013–3).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of FI-CL system. PMT: photomultiplier tube; BPCL: ultra weak luminescence analyzer.
CL of metal ions (0.1 mM) in PClCDs-H2O2 system (a), CL of fluorescent dyes (0.2 mM) in Fenton system and PClCDs-Fenton system (b).
CL spectra (a), effect of radical scavengers (b), spectra of excitation and emission of PClCDs, absorption and emission of EY (c).

head.ce:title: Synergistic enhanced of carbon dots and eosin Y on fenton chemiluminescence for the determination of methionine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041000, Shanxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041000, Shanxi, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ultra-weak chemiluminescence (CL) of Fenton system (Fe2+–H2O2) could be enhanced by phosphorus and chlorine co-doped carbon dots (PClCDs) or eosinY (EY), respectively. Surprisingly, the CL intensity of Fenton system was further improved in the co-presence of PClCDs and EY. The synergistic enhancing effect of PClCDs and EY on the CL emission of Fenton reaction provided a more strong and efficient CL system. It was found that methionine (Met) could effectively diminish the CL intensity of PClCDs-EY-Fenton system. Under the optimium conditions, a linear dynamic range of 0.1–10.0 μM Met concentrations was obtained, with limit of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) of 0.03 μM and 0.1 μM, respectively. The practical application of the presented CL system was demonstrated for detection of Met in food additive and feed additive samples. The mechanism of CL emission was described.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Phosphorus and chlorine co-doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Methionine (Met) is one of the essential amino acids to maintain the normal growth and development in human body. This essential role of Met is due to its involvement in several fundamental biologic processes, such as protein synthesis, transmethylation reaction, synthesis of cysteine and other metabolites of the transsulfuration pathway, and provision of homocysteine [1]. Met helps to reduce cholesterol level by increasing the lecithin production in liver, and can prevent disorders in hair, skin or nail. It is also a natural chelator for heavy metals, and a powerful antioxidant for free radicals scavenging. Deficiency of Met has been considered to cause various diseases such as toxaemia, acquired immune deficiency syndrome and Parkinson’s disease [2]. Aside from this, deficiency of Met in livestock and poultry can lead to dysplasia, weight loss, liver and kidney function damages, deterioration of hair quality, muscle atrophy. It is also a component of multivitamin and/or multiamino acid pharmaceutical preparations [3]. Some analytical techniques were reported for the determination of Met including colorimetry [4,5], photoelectrochemistry [6], fluorescence (FL) [7,8], electrochemistry [8-10], chemiluminescence (CL) [11], capillary electrophoresis [12], high performance liquid chromatography [13], and so on.

CL method has become a powerful analytical tool and has attracted significant attention because no excitation light is required and most samples have little or no unwanted background luminescence. In fact, most of the CL-based reactions have low quantum efficiency and hence produce a weak luminescence. However, the weak CL emission can be significantly enhanced by nanoparticles such as metal nanoparticles, semiconductor nanoparticles and graphene based nanostructures by virtue of their size-dependent and optical properties [14-18]. Fenton process is a strong oxidation system in which H2O2 is oxidant and Fe2+ is catalyst to produce highly active species, •OH, one of the most powerful oxidants known, triggering free radical chain reactions [19,20]. Fenton system (Fe2+–H2O2) has been studied extensively because of its fast reaction rate, simple operation and environmental friendliness, and has been successfully put into use for the degradation of diverse organic contaminants [19-22]. The ensuing research indicated that Fenton system may lead to a significant CL behavior [23]. However, the inherent drawback of weak light emission limited the applications of Fenton system in analytical chemistry. Therefore, some activators or sensitizer such as Eu(III) [24], Tb(III) [25], eosin Y (EY) [26] and nitrogen doped-carbon dots (N-CDs) [27,28] were used to increased the yield of CL of Fenton system.

Interesting luminescent properties, inexpensive and abundant raw materials, simple and low cost synthesis methods make carbon dots (CDs) widely used for analytical applications [17]. CDs can take part in the CL reactions as reductants, emitting species directly involved in redox oxidation, energy acceptors of CL energy transfer, or even catalysts involving other luminophors [29]. In fact, they always have more than one role in many cases owing to the formation of various excited species with short life in CL systems [30,31]. Recent reports have demonstrated that the introduction of N-CDs could improve the utilization of H2O2 and drastically enhance the generation of •OH, which resulted in enhanced CL emission of Fenton system through energy and electron transfer processes [27,28]. EY is an inexpensive and quite stable xanthene fluorescent dye with a multi-benzene rings structure and was chosen as the sensitizer for the determination of daidzein based on it reacted with the •OH from Fenton reagent [26].

Here, PClCDs were synthesized via a simple and green one pot aqueous hydrothermal approach using sucrose as carbon source, phosphoric acid and hydrochloric acid as providers of phosphorus and chlorine. Subsequent research showed that PClCDs or EY could promote the CL emission of Fenton reaction, respectively. However, the stronger increase of CL signal was obained only when PClCDs and EY co-existed in Fenton system. Furthermore, it was observed that Met manifested a significant CL quenching for the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system because Met could consume some free radicals. Therefore, a novel, simple, and sensitive flow injection (FI) CL system was developed for the determination of Met in feed and additive samples. The potential mechanism of the synergistic effect of PClCDs and EY on Fenton process was identified by CL, UV–vis, and FL spectra. The study of CL spectrum for PClCDs-EY-Fenton system showed that the ultimate emitting species was EY, and the main role of PClCDs was catalysis. Meanwhile, a CL resonance energy transfer (CRET) maybe occurred because the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system satisfied the condition of CRET. Therefore, the synergistic effect of PClCDs and EY made the Fenton system obtain a greater CL intensity. A schematic diagram for the PClCDs-EY-Fenton-Met system was showed in Scheme 1 .

All chemicals were analytically grade and was used without purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments. Sucrose, EY and Fe2SO4 were supplied from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Met was purchase form Nanjing Jingzhu Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was taken by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was performed by a Varian 660-IR spectrometer (Varian, USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectrum was acquired with using Ultima IV-185 X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). UV–vis spectrum was obtained using a TU-1810 PC UV–vis spectrophotometer the (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). FL spectrum was examined by a F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tianjin Port East Science and Technology Development Limited by Share Ltd, Tianjin, China). CL intensity-time curves were acquired by a flow injection (FI) CL system consisting of an IFIS-C model intelligent FI sampler (Xi’an Remax Analytical Instrument Co. Ltd., Shannxi, China) and a BPCL ultra-weak luminescence analyzer (Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing, China).

PClCDs were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal process according to the literature with some modifications [32]. Firstly, 0.5 g of sucrose, 0.15 mL of HCl, 0.15 mL of H3PO4 and 20 mL of water were placed in a beaker and stirred evenly. Then the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h in the blast drying oven. After the reaction, the reactor cooled to room temperature by naturally. The resulting dark yellow and transparent solution was subjected to dialysis in order to obtain the PClCDs. The synthesis method of P-doped CDs (PCDs) and Cl-doped CDs (ClCDs) was the same as that of PClCDs.

The configuration of the FI-CL system in this operation was shown in Fig. 1 . Two peristaltic pumps and a six-way injection valve were used to transport all the solution. The solutions of PClCDs and EY were pumped into the flow cell by a peristaltic pump 1 at the rate of 2.3 mL min−1. The Fe2+ solution, carrier H2O2 and standard or sample solution of Met were pumped by a peristaltic pump 2 at the rate of 2.5 mL min−1. The above solutions transported by two pumps were converged through a Y-shaped tube and then injected the flow CL reaction cell with the generation of CL signals. The CL signal was monitored by a photomultiplier tube (PMT, −800 V) adjacent to the flow CL reaction cell, and was directed to a computer for data acquisition. The determination of Met was based on the decrease of CL intensity (ΔI = I 0 − I s, where I 0 and I s were the CL intensity in absence and presence of Met, respectively). The CL kinetic characteristics of PClCDs were obtained by batch experiment, which was achieved by a static system consisted of a quartz glass cuvette and the CL analyzer.

Hydrothermal treatment of sucrose in phosphoric acid and hydrochloric acid solution at 180 °C for 6 h and further purification brought about a light yellow and transparent PClCDs solution. As seen in TEM image (Fig. S1a), the resultant PClCDs were nearly spherical morphology and were individually distributed without obvious aggregation. They exhibited a relatively narrow size range of 2.0–3.2 nm with an average diameter of about 2.65 nm, fitting well to the Gaussian function (inset of Fig. S1a). The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. S1a) showed that the PClCDs possessed the crystalline structure with lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which were very close to the (100) diffraction planes of graphite [33]. The XRD pattern (Fig. S1b) was showed one prominent peak at 24° which corresponded to the (002) planes of graphite. The FTIR spectrum was shown in Fig. S1c, a peak appeared at 2925 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of C–H. Two peaks at 1047 and 912 cm−1 were attributed to P–O and C–Cl vibrations, respectively, and confirmed the inclusion of phosphorus and chlorine in the PClCDs. The peak at 3431 cm−1 was ascribed to O–H. The stretching vibrations of C = O, C = C and C–O occurred at 1629 cm−1, 1384 cm−1 and 1083 cm−1, respectively. The above data indicated the formation of PClCDs with constituent elements of C, O, P, and Cl. The surface groups of PClCDs were also investigated by XPS (Fig. S1d). The XPS spectrum showed four dominant peaks at 533.08, 285.08, 198.08 and 132.08 eV, which were accompanying O 1s, C 1s, Cl 2p and P 2p, respectively. On C1s spectrum, four absorption peaks (Fig. S1e) were found at 288.5, 287.4, 286.1 and 284.6 eV, which corresponded to C = O, C–Cl, C–O, and C = C bonds, respectively. The Cl 2p spectrum disclosed two peaks at 199.8 and 198.1 eV (Fig. S1f), which were assigned to the Cl 2p 1/2 and Cl 2p 3/2 bonding [34]. Fig. S1g showed the P 2p spectrum had two peaks centred at 133.4 and 132.4 eV, indicating the presence of P as P–C and P–O [32,35]. The spectra of FTIR and XPS indicated that some hydroxyl, carboxylic and carbonyl groups were generated on the surface of the PClCDs.

In the UV–vis spectrum of PClCDs, two weak peaks were focused on 250 nm and 350 nm (Fig. S2a), repectively. The PClCDs displayed two FL emission peaks at 330–410 nm excitation wavelengths (Fig. S2b) and their FL emission of both peaks showed the excitation-dependent behavior, this was common in FL carbon materials [36]. The position of the first peak changed obviously and the second peak changed slightly. The intensity of the peak increased firstly and then decreased with increasing excitation wavelength. Combined with the FL spectra of PCDs (Fig. S3a) and ClCDs (Fig. S3b), it was possible that P element dopant was more absorptive at a lower wavelength while Cl element dopant at a high wavelength. In addition, the FL intensity of PClCDs exhibited a change with pH, the maximum FL intensity was obtained at pH = 4, whereas at higher pH, the FL intensity significantly decreased (Fig. S2c). The PL spectra of PCDs and ClCDs were also measured and compared with that of PClCDs. Fig. S3a revealed that the position and intensity of the emission peak of PCDs changed slowly as the excitation wavelength increased, and two emission peaks for ClCDs (Fig. S3b) were observed with varying excitation wavelengths. According to the report, Cl-doping could introduce additional energy levels, effectively creating more electron transition pathways in the band structure of CDs [34]. Based on these results, it was reasonable to deduce that the doped Cl was the main reason for the result of double FL peaks of PClCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of of PClCDs-enhanced CL system by FI mode
ce:section.ce:para: FI system can control the flow rate, mixing time and volume of reaction reagent precisely, which was used to study the CL behaviors of several systems. The CL reaction of PClCDs with some common oxidants was preliminarily investigated and compared [37–39]. As shown in Fig. S4, all oxidants were able to directly elicit CL of different intensity with PClCDs, and the CL produced with H2O2 in alkaline medium was more intensive than that of other oxidants. However, when it was used for analytical application, the CL intensity was still a little low. Next, some measures must be taken to improve the CL intensity of the PClCDs-H2O2 system. As everyone knows, transition metal ions such as Fe, Cu and Co can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2, producing reactive oxygen species and accompaning a CL emission. This is well the famous Fenton or Fenton-like reaction. Therefore, the effect of some metal ions on the PClCDs-H2O2 system was also studied (Fig. 2 a). The results showed that Fe2+ could remarkably enhance the CL signal of the reaction, while other metal ions exhibited much lower effect in a suitable medium. And then some fluorescent dyes such as EY, rhodamine 6G (Rh6G), rhodamine B (RhB), dichlorofluorescein (DFl) and fluorescein (Fl) were tested as sensitizers for the PClCDs-Fenton system (Fig. 2b). It was observed that the strongest CL emission occurred in the presence of EY, and the CL intensity was increased by more than 20 times compared with the PClCDs-Fenton system. In this way, a PClCDs-EY-Fenton CL system was initially formed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL from PClCDs-EY-Fenton system by batch experiments
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetic curves obtained by batch experiments were used to study the process of CL system, as presented in Fig. 3 a. No CL signal was observed for the PClCDs-Fe2+ system and a weak CL signal was obtained for Fenton system. A higher CL intensity was observed for the PClCDs-Fenton system or the EY-Fenton system and the strongest CL occurred for the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system.

The CL dynamic curves of four reagent injection orders were obtained by batch experiment. In mode 1, PClCDs were injected into the mixture of EY-Fenton, which resulted in very little increment in CL signal. From mode 2, no obvious CL signal was observed when EY was injected into the mixture of PClCDs-Fenton. Fig. 3b showed that EY-Fenton system or PClCDs-Fenton system had a high baseline and unstable signals. This maybe due to some reactions occurred in EY-Fenton system or PClCDs-Fenton system before the addition of PClCDs or EY. The highest increment in CL signal was obtained by mode 4, viz., H2O2 injected to the mixture of PClCDs-EY-Fe2+. The CL of mode 3, namely, Fe2+ injected to the mixture of PClCDs-EY-H2O2, was lower than that of mode 4 (Fig. 3c). This maybe due to no obvious reaction occurred before H2O2 or Fe2+ was injected into the PClCDs-EY-Fe2+ system or the PClCDs-EY-H2O2 system, which could be explained by the very low baseline signal value of these systems before injecting H2O2 or Fe2+.

The flow path of the FI-CL analysis was desinged according to the mode 4. In this mode, CL signals reached to its maximum intensity in 2 s followed by a slow decrease. The abrupt increase was due to the fast reaction between Fe2+ and H2O2 and the equilibrium between Fe2+ and Fe3+ resulted in slow decrease in CL intensity [27].

The CL properties of PClCDs, PCDs and ClCDs in EY-Fenton systyem was compared (Fig. S5), and the highest intensity was observed when PClCDs appeared in EY-Fenton systyem. This may be due to the fact that the FL emission band of PClCDs was wider than that of PCDs or ClCDs, which was conducive to better overlap with the absorption spectrum of EY, more energy transfer, more EY (EY*) formation, and then the system naturally obtained stronger CL signal [29]. A control experiment was carried out as the employed precursors of PClCDs synthesis. The results showed that the mixture of sucrose, HCl and H3PO4 solutions had no significant effect on the enhancement of CL intensity.

In order to obtain an insight into the nature of the emitting species involved in the PClCDs-EY-Fenton CL reaction, CL spectra of related sysyems were measured using a series of high-energy cut off filters (Fig. 4 a). The Fenton reaction was investigated in the presence of PClCDs (curve 1), EY(curve 2) or mixing PClCDs and EY (curve 5), respectively. The result indicated that the CL emission band and CL peak of three systems were similar, their emission band was in the range of 490–600 nm and peak was located at 535 nm. Therefore, the emitter in the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system maybe attributed to the excited-state PClCDs (PClCDs*) and EY*. In order to further confirm which one was the main emitter, Rh6G and mixing PClCDs-Rh6G were also added into the Fenton reaction for comparative study (curve 3, 4), respectively. Whether Rh6G or mixing PClCDs-Rh6G appeared in the Fenton reaction, CL peaks were all at 555 nm, which was identical to the maximum FL emission of Rh6G. This indicated that the luminor in Rh6G-Fenton system and PClCDs-Rh6G-Fenton system was excited state Rh6G. This simple experiment strongly demonstrated that the main emitter was EY in the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system, just as the emitter was Rh6G in the PClCDs-Rh6G-Fenton system. This was also showed the catalytic effect of PClCDs in the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system.

To study the intermediates generated in the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system, the effects of radical scavengers on its CL intensity were investigated [40,41] and the results were summarized in Table S1 and Fig. 4b. As a classical reducing agent and common free radical scavenger, the effect of ascorbic acid on CL intensity was studied. Its inhibition on CL signal confirmed that the radical process of the CL reaction. The •OH plays an important role in Fenton reaction, of course, it’s just as important in the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system. The generation of •OH was confirmed from the significant quenching effect of thiourea, an effective •OH scavenger. Nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) is frequently used to detect the •O2 –, and a great inhibition of NBT for CL intensity was noted. This supported the speculation of •O2 – existing in the system [42]. NaN3 quenches 1O2 through physical manner [41]. The addition of NaN3 resulted in a smaller decrease of the CL intensity, which was a indication that a small amount of 1O2 was generated in the CL system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of PClCDs or EY in Fenton reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
A simple chemical system consisting of Fe2+ and H2O2, viz, Fenton reaction, was shown to emit weak CL, and a radical chain reaction involving •OH, •O2 − and 1O2 was responsible for the CL reaction [23,43]. The weak CL from Fenton reaction was boosted in the presence of PClCDs. The PClCDs might act as catalyst, energy acceptor as well as taking part in redox reaction to enhance the CL of Fenton system [27,28]. The enhancement was first attributed to the catalytic behavior of PClCDs, which was assumed to facilitate radical generation and accelerate the electron transfer process. PClCDs as a catalyst facilitated decomposition of H2O2 to generate •OH and •O2 −, which resulted in 1O2 on the surface of PClCDs [44]. The generated 1O2 transferred its energy to PClCDs forming PClCDs* (Eq. (1)), which acted as emitter in the enhanced Fenton CL system [45]. Likewise, •O2 − injected electron in the conduction band of PClCDs to form negatively charged PClCDs (PClCDs•–) (Eq. (2)), while •OH injected hole in the valence band of PClCDs to form positively charged PClCDs (PClCDs•+) (Eq. (3)) [46], and PClCDs* were formed through an electron-transfer annihilation effect (Eq. (4)). Therefore, enhanced CL emission occurred in the process of PClCD* deexcitation (Eq. (5)).
(1)
1O2 + PClCDs → PClCDs*+ O2
(2) PClCDs + •O2 – → PClCDs•–
(3) PClCDs + •OH → PClCDs•+
(4) PClCDs•+ + PClCDs•– → PClCDs*
(5) PClCDs* →PClCDs + hν (λ = 535 nm)

When EY was added into the Fenton system, the CL signal was enhanced greatly and the color of EY decolorized gradually [26]. It may be that EY was oxidized by •OH generated in Fenton reaction and released a certain amount of energy (Eq. (6)), which was absorbed by the unreacted EY to form EY* (Eq. (7)), emitting its characteristic light at 535 nm once EY* returned to the ground state (Eq. (8)).
(6) EY + •OH → product + energy
(7) EY + energy → EY*
(8) EY*→EY + hv (λ = 535 nm)
The FL spectra of PClCDs with or without Fenton (Fig. S6a), and the absorption spectra of EY with or without Fenton (Fig. S6b) showed that PClCDs or EY was involved in the Fenton reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic effect of PClCDs and EY for Fenton CL enhancement
ce:section.ce:para:
Compared with PClCDs-Fenton or EY-Fenton system, the stronger CL appeared in PClCDs-EY-Fenton system based on the synergistic effect of PClCDs and EY on Fenton reaction. The CL spectra revealed that the ultimate main emitter in the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system was EY. Many literatures reported that nanostructures are found to possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity [46,47], which can catalyze the oxidation of H2O2. Therefore, PClCDs could serve as a catalyzer to accelerate the decomposition of H2O2 that lead to an increase in the number of •OH (Eq. (9)). Consequently, the more EY was oxidized by •OH, the more energy was released and the more EY* was increased and the CL intensity was enhanced. Meanwhile, the PClCDs and EY could participate in energy transfer as an energy donor and acceptor, giving rise to the formation of EY* (Eq. (10)). Ultimately, reforming the ground state of EY could emanate emissions around 535 nm.
(9) H2O2 + PClCDs (catalyst) → 2•OH
(10) PClCDs* + EY → PClCDs + EY*

Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) is described as the nonradiative energy sharing from a CL reaction (donor) to a fluorophore (acceptor) with a high quantum yield [48]. The essential condition for CRET is an overlap between CL spectrum and absorption spectrum of FL acceptor. PClCDs were well suited FL acceptors in CRET process due to their broad excitation spectra (Fig. 4c). Therefore, primary energy transfer from 1O2 (donor) to PClCDs (acceptor) may be possible. Meanwhile, it was clear from the Fig. 4c that there was a considerable intersection between FL emission band of PClCDs (donor) and abosorption band of EY(acceptor) which resulted in secondary CRET occurrence. As shown in Fig. S6c, when PClCDs and different amounts of EY were mixed, FL intensity of PClCDs decreased and FL intensity of EY increased, this further confirmed that CRET between PClCDs and EY really occurred.

In short, the CL intensity of Fenton system was greatly improved based on the catalysis, sensitization, CRET and synergistic effect of PClCDs and EY, which made the Fenton system suitable for analytical application.

It was found that Met had the quenching effect on the CL intensity of PClCDs-EY-Fenton system (Fig. 4b). •OH produced in situ through the Fenton process is one of the most powerful oxidants known, and it could destroy Met by redox reactions. So •OH and possibly reactive species from the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system were consumed in the presence of Met, resulting in the weakening of the CL intensity. Table S1 showed that the presence of Met increased the inhibition for CL signal of the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system.

In order to obtain the maximum sensitivity for the introduced reaction, the effects of the concentrations of PClCDs, EY, H2O2, Fe2+ and H2SO4 were investigated (Fig. S7). The following experimental conditions were found to give best results: 0.05 M PClCDs, 0.2 mM EY, 0.25 M H2O2, 5.0 mM Fe2+ and 5.0 mM H2SO4.

The CL intensity of PClCDs-EY-Fenton system was decreased with increase of Met concentration in the range of 0.1–10.0 μM. The regression equation was ΔI = 322.48[Met] + 24.35 with a correlation coefficient (R) of 0.9992 (Fig. S8). The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were taken to be 3-fold and 10-fold of the standard deviation in the blank solution, respectively [27]. The LOD was 0.03 μM, (LOD = 3 s/k), while LOQ was 0.1 μM, (LOQ = 10 s/k). Where“s” is standard deviation of blank and “k” is slope of calibration graph. To evaluate the precision of the method, a set of 11 measurements of standard 1.0 μM Met solution was carried out. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 0.55% (intra-day) and 2.0% (inter-day, repeatedly for 5 consecutive days), respectively. From the analytical point of view, the developed method allowed the result to be obtained with high precision. These results showed that PClCDs-EY-Fenton system has very promising application in the detection of Met.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the proposed CL system
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system toward Met was analyzed with respect to other compounds. Fig. S9 showed that the inhibition of Met was stronger, tryptophane and lysine were slight and others were ignored. This indicated that the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system could be used for the selective determiantion of Met. Moreover, the selectivity of the PClCDs-EY-Fenton system was also estimated for 1.0 μM Met in the presence of various quantities of the predicted interfering species. The compound amount causing relative error less than 5% was taken as tolerance point (Table S2). The results indicated no significant interference from the tested species.

The method was applied for the determination of Met in food additive and feed additive samples, which were purchased from local market. The powder samples were accurately weighed and dissolved with water in the calibrated flask to stock solution, which was sonicated for 10 min to make complete dissolution. The appropriate working solution was prepared by quantitatively diluting the stock solution. The quantity of Met in the spiked samples was obtained by a standard addition method, and the results were summarized in Table S3. The recovery was measured to assess the accuracy of the developed CL method. The recovery of two additives was between 97% and 102%, and RSD ranged from 2.64% to 3.13%. Therefore, the presented method could be used for the determination of Met in additive samples with good accuracy.

It was found that PClCDs or EY could effectively promote the weak CL emission of Fenton reaction. This promoting effect was tremendously improved in the co-presence of PClCDs and EY based on the catalytic effect of PClCDs, sensitizing effect of EY and synergistic effect of PClCDs and EY on the Fenton system. Consequently, an intense and efficient CL system was introduced based on PClCDs-EY-Fenton assembly. Moreover, Met showed a specific quenching on the presented CL system and the decrease of CL intensity was proportional with Met concentration in the wide linear range with a LOD of 0.03 μM. As a result, Met concentrations were satisfactorily assessed in food additive and feed additive samples using the introduced CL method in this study.
Yaqiong Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Suqin Han: Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 2013011013–3).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105902.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
